_comment: migration-data.json — Partition of British India 1947. PLACEHOLDER DATA. Replace with verified historical figures from primary sources (Schechtman 1963, 1951 Census of India, 1951 Pakistan Census). All population figures are approximations.

sources: Schechtman, J.B. (1963). The Refugee in the World.
Brass, P. (2003). The Production of Hindu-Muslim Violence in Contemporary India.

regions.name: West Punjab / East Punjab
regions.description: The most violent zone of displacement. Lahore went to Pakistan; Amritsar stayed in India. The Radcliffe Line divided communities that had coexisted for centuries.

regions.name: East Bengal / West Bengal
regions.description: Bengal was divided between Pakistan (East Bengal / later Bangladesh) and India (West Bengal). Calcutta remained in India; Dhaka became the capital of East Pakistan.

regions.name: Sindh
regions.description: Karachi became Pakistan's first capital. Most of Sindh's Hindu merchant class — who had dominated trade for generations — fled to Bombay.

cities.name: Saidpur
cities.note: Hill station NW of Rawalpindi. Hindu/Sikh population fled Aug 1947.

flows.description: Hindu and Sikh families fleeing West Punjab toward India
Muslim families fleeing East Punjab toward Pakistan
Hindu families from East Bengal moving to West Bengal
Muslim families from West Bengal moving to East Pakistan
Hindu merchants and traders leaving Karachi and Sindh for Bombay/Delhi
